title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for two road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that may now move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included updates on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, Scofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and a special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue scheduled for May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Michael reported updated modeling from Strand shows the pond will remove more phosphorus for Weston but less for Rothschild. Staff anticipates a possible amendment to the cost-sharing agreement. They also broke out the DOT's portion hoping the state will buy in for about a quarter of the project cost.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, spring curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from East Nick. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring three 18-inch pipes. Equipment maintenance included removing plow wings and brine systems from trucks, servicing mowers and tractors, and preparing units for summer use. A new MAC tandem axle chassis has been ordered.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and continued private well sampling. Sanitary side experienced significant clear water infiltration - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into distribution but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers as a major contributor. An INI study is planned to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. Utility value reached nearly $34 million, an increase of 6.7% due to infrastructure additions including Wells 7 and 8. The utility serves 6,100 customers total with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Approved unanimously.

The League of Wisconsin Municipalities drafted this sample resolution for communities to adopt supporting long-term funding solutions for the state transportation fund. Michael noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and recurring annual funding for maintenance and snow plowing remains inadequate. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel was low bidder at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed village savings of approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, saving about $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

A builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 at 2230 Radkey Avenue. Staff proposed allowing temporary long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, when the owner would pay assessment for main extension. Committee amended to require reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for water and sewer utilities is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction will hold the contract. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek intersection roundabout: MSA at $37,315 (recommended) vs Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: MSA at $70,400 vs Clark Dietz at $73,730. Staff recommended Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than summer interns. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Enter agreement with 2230 Radkey Avenue property owner for temporary utility connection with assessment due upon reconstruction no later than 2029
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for Weston utilities at estimated $94,860
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services
Norcon to begin Scofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday this week
Water main flushing to begin next week - notices going out to residents

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously without opposition.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned the room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved $5,000 (same as last year), with a request that organizers obtain accurate room usage data from hotels for future reports. Motion passed unanimously with Jackson seconding.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia sat at their table. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide accurate hotel room usage data from hotels in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program - Wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jennifer from staff explained the building meets all B2 zoning requirements and landscaping meets current code. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline easement, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building will have capacity for 50-60 officers versus current staffing of 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. Discussion included questions about future expansion capability (possible to north), an alternate storage shed placement, and gas pipeline considerations. Motion to approve was made and seconded, passing unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities within wellhead protection zones - a warm weather inspection for structural integrity and a winter operational inspection. Jennifer presented a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Michael noted the village's wells are dispersed across four distinct areas making them more susceptible to contamination. Motion to approve with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the certified survey maps/site plans and building permits for April 2026 were approved unanimously with minimal discussion.

Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email invitations and calendar events.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing the Green Heck Turner building situation, to avoid excessive taxpayer costs. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on visits and findings for sites in wellhead protection zones.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings back to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts with commissioners
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in future agenda (likely fall)

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new and returning members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received comprehensive overviews of each department's mission, budget structure, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (remote participation noted)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance 2.04 sub 5 - missions, duties, responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Services Department presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date confirmation and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting aye.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under ordinance 2.04 sub 5, including oversight of Veteran Services, Social Services, Health Department, North Central Health Care, and ADRC. He detailed the committee's relationships with statutory bodies including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, Board of Health, and North Central Healthcare Board.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease and protecting public health. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, infectious disease monitoring, Nurse Family Partnership program now graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new homeless system response position. The department has 37.6 FTE and approximately half of its $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant intergovernmental grant revenue.

Administrator Leonard covered the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing root causes of housing instability. The department has nearly 140 FTE with a $22 million budget, of which approximately $7 million is tax levy.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. The department now has 3.0 FTE. Galindo discussed initiatives including surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability efforts with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention planning noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county regional ADRC model serving Lincoln, Langlade, Marathon, and Wood counties. He described services including information assistance, Meals on Wheels, caregiver support, dementia care, and falls prevention. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% while federal funding remained flat since 2024. The organization has about 60 FTE and relies on 385-400 volunteers.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate full presentation before committee
Next meeting confirmed for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Strategic plan review to be presented at upcoming meeting
ADRC Executive Director to present annual report with 2025 data to full board

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinance amendments including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park following the loss of their previous location. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Unfinished business - remote meeting attendance policy amendments
New business - committee appointments and secondhand dealer license
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 per single family unit to $761, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed operational data to justify the specific fee amounts. Trustee Motti supported the fees as justified given population growth. Both Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 were approved unanimously.

Parks and Recreation reported playground mats installed at Yellow Banks and trees removed from the river. Public Works discussed upcoming recycling and inspection contracts. Tourism reported on grants awarded: $2,500 to GG's, $10,000 to Wausau Mum Festival, $5,000 to Mountain Bay Cup, and $5,000 offered to Wisconsin Valley Fair. Finance committee learned about clerk department costs for elections and newsletters. The MPD joint finance committee voted to disperse funds back to the three communities (Schofield, Town of Weston, Village of Weston).

Significant debate occurred over policy language. The board voted to strike sentences requiring specific justifications (health, safety, welfare) for remote attendance, making the policy more broad as discussed at the previous meeting. The amended policy requires 12-hour advance notice, camera must be on, and remote attendance is not permitted for closed sessions. Amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Erling and Motti. Final amended policy passed with two no votes from the same trustees.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion approved unanimously. The board agreed to include committee/commission applicant resumes in future meeting packets for transparency.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional inspection sticker system with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and other municipalities. Once a food truck is inspected by any participating department, a sticker indicates compliance so it doesn't need re-inspection in each community. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article Dealer License - Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: The board discussed the purpose of secondhand dealer licensing as a safety measure to track potentially stolen merchandise. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar amounts. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

Extensive discussion about the market's relocation to Kennedy Park after losing their previous location at Camp Phillips and Schofield Avenue when the property owner decided to develop. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson. Renee provided history: original site had 25 stalls, Camp Phillips had 42 stalls without vehicle parking, Kennedy Park now has 53 spots with vendor vehicle parking. The board discussed that Mont Miller Park would have poor visibility. Renee confirmed the market operates at no loss to the village, with grant funding covering token programs and vendor fees covering operational costs.

Public Works Director Michael reported that engineering study showed 35 mph, but traffic data showed 85th percentile speed at 45 mph. A second speed study is needed but was delayed due to construction. Trustee Pitel noted during discussion that the current speed is inconsistent with ordinance and needs resolution. Staff will bring back to the board.

Michael addressed public comment about the unfilled engineering technician position, explaining it requires a very specific skill set that is scarce in central Wisconsin. Even consulting firms are pulling staff from southeast Wisconsin offices. Two outside engineering contracts totaling approximately $108,000 were discussed for current projects while position remains unfilled.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5902 Street spoke criticizing the board for hiring outside engineering consultants at $108,000 instead of filling the vacant engineering technician position, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting before official board appointment, complained about lack of Kennedy Park information in the meeting packet, and criticized the administrator's accountability. He also spoke during the park impact fee public hearing questioning the lack of operational data to justify specific fee amounts.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased to $761 single family, $563 duplex, $495 multifamily effective immediately
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive Plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Electronics drive rescheduled to June 6th
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Committee/commission applicant resumes to be included in future meeting packets
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Discussion of increasing tourism fund contribution to aquatic center (currently $40,000) to be placed on future agenda

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected two at-large executive committee members (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and unanimously passed a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris related to the recent severe weather and tornado damage in Ringle and other affected areas. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution, which reduces fees from the standard $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified storm-related debris.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Election of at-large executive committee members (Item 9B)
Resolution approving out-of-home placement reserve increase (D15)
Resolution to amend social services budget (D16)
Resolution approving modification to landfill tipping fees (E17)
Vote on suspension of rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing leadership opportunities for newer supervisors (34% of the board is in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. The appointments were approved by motion from Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Hendricks noted he was marked absent but didn't get the option to vote and wanted to be recorded as voting aye.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large executive committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she has seen her advocate for the county's smallest children and oldest elders, bringing a voice to underrepresented populations. Paper ballots were distributed and counted, resulting in Supervisors Hapa and Arstead being elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved by motion from Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.
Resolution from Health and Human Services and HR Finance and Property committees was moved by Supervisor Morash and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the emergency resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board responding to severe tornado and flood damage in Ringle and other areas. The proposal reduces tipping fees from the standard $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified storm debris, with the state waiving $13.10 and Ringle waiving $2.33 of their fees. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county wave fees entirely for affected residents. Multiple supervisors asked questions about the fee structure, timeline, and policy implications. Administrator Leonard noted the county will not meet the $670,743.18 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle township to meet their damage threshold. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then voted unanimously to approve the resolution.

publicComment: Special permission was granted for two residents to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty spoke for three minutes, requesting Marathon County wave its fees entirely for disaster debris, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees. She mentioned one business has spent $40,000 on cleanup efforts and multiple businesses have donated time and resources. She requested a six-month timeline for fee waivers. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 affected homeowners, noting many are still waiting for insurance adjusters and requesting extended relief beyond the proposed timeline due to uncertainty about insurance coverage.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approving out-of-home placement reserve increase approved
Resolution 25-26 amending social services budget approved
Resolution 26-26 modifying landfill tipping fees for storm debris approved through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling disaster-related debris within six months
Staff to track certified storm-related debris and apply reduced rates accordingly

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems on properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received department overviews from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Garrett Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make the existing structures conforming uses rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve and forward to county board, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-damaged properties in the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to property owners affected by smaller disasters not declared emergencies. After significant debate, Supervisor Sefelt moved to forward the resolution, seconded by Supervisor Leur. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to only waive reconnect fees for septic systems (not other zoning or septic fees), seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The amendment passed. The amended motion to forward to county board passed but was not unanimous.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented on solid waste operations, explaining the landfill accepts about 1,000 tons daily, has partnerships with Portage and Shawano counties, and began injecting renewable natural gas into pipelines in September 2025 through a partnership with Vidi Energy. He highlighted the upcoming $6 million 11-acre construction project and the need for on-site leachate treatment by 2032 due to new state requirements (Acts 200 and 201). Marilyn Bend praised the department for helping keep private landfill costs in line.

discussions.item: Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He explained the department operates both county and city park systems with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the Marathon County economy. Tom announced his retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years with the county. Supervisor Sefelt praised the department and Tom's service.

actionItems: Rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) approved and forwarded to county board
Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) approved and forwarded to county board
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) for tornado-damaged properties forwarded to county board for consideration
Towns should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of kayak launch signs for the Plover River Water Trail and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until the next meeting when the committee chair can participate. The meeting also featured updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and addressed concerns about graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion was held.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline document tracing the Kennedy Park project from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the recent ribbon cutting on May 9th. He explained that the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for lights on the upper field, having raised approximately $84-85,000 toward a $117,000 goal. Sean clarified that the $8,700 in revenue shown was a Youth Baseball contribution for equipment, not Friends group money. Committee member Scott asked about village capital funds, and Sean confirmed no village funds are currently earmarked pending fundraising progress. The committee discussed that the 10-year timeline is flexible and plans may evolve.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Sean presented photos and maps showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during logging equipment access. Dan was present to provide additional details about the mat installation process and pipeline crossings.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to the next meeting because Committee Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy (6504 [street name inaudible]) spoke during public comment opposing the idea, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The committee assured her the objection would be noted and she could return next month.

Sean explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches, noting their pressure washer cannot access the trail due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Committee members discussed anti-vandalism coatings and seal options. Scott suggested organizing volunteer workdays since the trail is well-loved by the community. Sean noted that the benches were originally installed through a volunteer team-building day with village directors. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan Kroening (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of the Plover River Water Trail signage design. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs are funded through Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants at no cost to the village. Dan noted the Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. The committee approved a motion to install the signs and forward to the village board. Motion passed unanimously.

Sean raised a request from a community member about installing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds, noting statistics that 1 in 13 people have food allergies. The committee agreed to bring it back for discussion. The Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler consultants, and a bike and pedestrian plan open house is scheduled for June 11th.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street name inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools and 'We have rivers. We have lakes. That's where they belong.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street name] Street, Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the exact $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and Glow event proceeds are being tracked. He stated the numbers are 'extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Approve and install Plover River Water Trail kayak launch signs at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks landings - forward to village board
Defer dog day at aquatic center discussion to June meeting
Staff to explore cleaning and anti-vandalism coating options for Oaklair River Trail benches and surfaces
Bring food allergy awareness playground signage proposal back for future discussion
Begin Mock Miller Park master plan process with Rettler consultants kickoff meeting
Friends of Kennedy Park to present fundraising check at June tournament

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged monthly financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

A committee member asked about final numbers for TIF closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, noting the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Staff confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt. The bonds are due October 1st.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on the clerk's office operations with staff members Sarah (full-time) and Leah (part-time). She explained the office went from four full-time staff to currently two and a half positions. Key points included: elections administration with 13 Badger Books being replaced four at a time at $11,000 for four units; 51 total liquor licenses processed; 84% of tax collection ($22 million) handled by walk-ins at the clerk's office; pet licensing brought back in-house after 12 years with Pet Data saving roughly $5,495 annually; and the quarterly newsletter costing approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to 5,089 postal patrons.

Clerk Pam noted that based on a Marathon County survey of 52 municipalities, Weston's chief inspector pay of $13/hour is on the lower end. She plans to request an increase to $15/hour for the next election term due to the responsibilities involved.

Discussion occurred about the pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 for neutered animals. Pam explained Marathon County requested increases because they pay about $100,000 to the Humane Society annually but were only collecting $65,000 from municipalities.

Committee discussed the Mountain Bay Metro contract for non-dog strays. It was noted that eight mice were picked up last year at approximately $175 per mouse. A committee member suggested requiring law enforcement to bring in animals rather than allowing residents to bring them directly, which other municipalities with similar contracts require.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well, with full vendor spots including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. Some concerns were raised about parking on Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified only the west side of Alderson allows parking; the east side remains no parking.
Evan announced this was his last meeting as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated summer 2025
Clerk's office seeking to hire one more part-time employee
Clerk plans to request chief inspector wage increase to $15/hour for next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized razing of a condemned property at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also heard extensive public comment criticizing the new farmers market location and discussed overnight parking regulations, deferring that item for further staff research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledge department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance

discussions.body: The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50 regarding nuisances, including a previously discussed change to tree limb heights over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet, with compliance to jurisdiction authority). Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance for traffic and vehicles. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways and sidewalks, citing safety concerns. Discussion also touched on whether electric scooters used by children should be exempted, with consensus to leave enforcement discretion to officers. Trustee Olsen motioned to recommend approval with the addition of prohibiting emotos from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Seconded by Sherwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most use 4-5 hour windows). Michael from Public Works noted the current system was designed for daytime plowing operations. Chief Hunt advocated keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific problem areas for potential signage before the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk on Alderson Street between the church and DC Everest high school parking lot. Staff estimated cost at $2,000-3,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for a lighted system. The committee agreed they would not fund it but would approve installation if Mount Olive covers the cost. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive paying for installation. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendors Fire Inspection Ordinance
discussions.body: Chief presented Ordinance 26-8 creating a regional fire inspection program for mobile food vendors, partnering with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer. Inspections by any participating department would be valid regionwide. Chief noted that Fire Inspector Marty already inspected one food truck that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane cylinders. Wausau opted out to maintain their own inspection system. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections: 5915 Delicowski Street (discovered to be a duplex, will become 5915 and 5917), Ross Avenue properties requiring resequencing due to apartment discovery, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing correction for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski as needing review (8 apartments with 9 separate addresses). Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and was previously considered for a fire department training burn but deemed too unsafe. Staff discovered chickens being housed inside the condemned structure. Current owners are up to date on taxes and have indicated preference for the raze order process. Demolition costs will be added to the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Code Enforcement reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement list after sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, 281 citations, and announced hiring of the 40th officer. Two SROs work with zoning enforcement during summer, including pet licensing compliance. Motion by Sherwanka to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7, seconded by Olsen. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke extensively criticizing the new farmers market location at the park, stating his opinion that it is 'a terrible execution' and predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed by end of summer. He raised concerns about parking vehicles on grass potentially violating village code, ADA-accessible parking being placed on grass instead of asphalt, the addition of fire inspections for food trucks questioning if it was necessary, and ambiguity in mobile food vendor ordinance language regarding which days prohibitions apply. He also criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, noting some have been pending for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to Board of Trustees with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Michael to research and identify specific problem areas for overnight parking signage for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees for E911 compliance
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
